# Transcrição: 04-Anatomy of an Email

- **Fonte:** 04-Anatomy of an Email
- **Duração:** 12:24
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:01]

All right, let's talk about the anatomy of a great email. Now I want you to think about an email as you send it. Think about the emails that you get in your inbox and what is the goal of these emails? The goal is to get the email opened. Then the goal is to get the person to start reading the email. Then the next goal is to get them to keep reading the email. Then finally our goal is to get them to click through that email. We have four goals with our emails. I want to tell you what you can put in each part of the email to be sure that more and more people achieve that final goal. The last thing we want to happen is for your people to open their email, see an email from you and hit the delete button. Instead, we want them to go, I got an email from her. I've got to open this thing and see what she has to say. That comes from creating great structure with your email. First and most important, the most, most, most important part of your email is the subject line. Dean and Tony talk about this, the hook. The hook is the most important of all your messaging, but it is critical for your emails because this will determine whether someone presses delete or spam or unsubscribe or they open that email and start to read it. Here are a couple of things you want to keep in mind when you think about your subject line. First, the word curious, curiosity. You want that subject line to invoke curiosity with your reader. The minute they read your lines, you want them to think, what's she saying? I wonder what the answer to that is. I wonder what's inside. We want a little bit of a prompt to let the person have to open the email to get the answer to that curiosity. You can also use the idea of a subject line being a little bit confusing. Put something in the subject line that would make the reader go, huh, what is she talking about? What does she mean? I've got to open this email and figure that out. Or you can make your subject line confrontational. You can make a strong statement like stop doing this or this has got to stop or I hope you're not doing this. Something that evokes a little bit of a defense mechanism in your reader is going to get them to open that email. You also could use a subject line that is extremely confident. This is my favorite. This is the only. This is the best. I will never use anything else. If you start with subject lines like that, it catches the attention of your reader. It catches the attention of them when they're scrolling through their inbox and deciding if they're deleting and swiping and spamming or if they're opening and reading. So the subject line is the most important.

## [03:01 - 06:02]

You should spend the majority of your time when you're writing your emails on crafting an incredible subject line. In fact, it's a great practice to write three to five, even 10 subject lines when you're first getting started and test these first for yourself and then for your teammates or for your family members. Anyone that will read these subject lines, send them over and have them pick the one that would make them open the email fastest. The next thing that is most important after subject is the pre-header. This is the little tagline, the little teaser for the entire email that will be shown in most people's inboxes when they open up their email. So they'll see the subject nice and bold and then they'll see a few lines of the pre-header. Now some things you want to keep in mind for the pre-header. Again, this is the second most important part of your entire email is you want to keep it personal. So this is a place to kind of use some friendly language, some language that maybe you would send to your best friend, to your mom, to your sister. You also want this pre-header to sound a little promising, like open up, this is going to be inside or you don't want to miss this or this is the best thing you're going to see in your inbox today. So something that makes the person say, I got to at least check it out and see if she is good on her word, right? If she said this is the best thing, I've got to at least open and see if she's right. You also could use your pre-header to be open-ended. So what we're always thinking about in email is getting them to take the next step, right? We want them first to open the email, then we want them to start to read the email. And so this pre-header, if you make it open-ended, if it has a little, what is that called? A little ellipsis at the end, the three dots, I think I'm right. My grammar teacher might be disappointed. So three dots, something like that, have a little question mark, something that has the reader go, oh, I think she got cut off or something. She's got something else to say. I've got to open the email and see what that is. So we've got our subject, most important. This is the hook for your email. We've got our pre-header. This is when we bring in some personality, some promise, and we try to tease the email just a little bit so that they have got to take the next step and open that email. Next up, we've got our third most important part of the email, and this is the opening. So with your opening, I know if you're anything like me, when I first started writing emails, I would just do a slow introduction, tell a little bit about what's going on, and start the email almost like you would start a once upon a time kind of story. Have you done that? I know I have. Well, in your openings, I want you to switch it up. And instead of starting with a once upon a time, instead of starting with an in the beginning, I want you to start your opening right in the middle of the story, right in the middle of the action.

## [06:02 - 09:05]

So we want your readers to feel like when they open their inbox, they call it a movie like right in the climax of the story. They opened a book and they went right to the point where the guy was about to lose his life. We want to go right to the key part of the action at the start of our email. Now, you can do this with quotes where something very powerful was said. You can do this simply by describing something that was very dramatic, but you want it to be somewhere in the middle of the story rather than the beginning because what we want to do again is get them to go to the next step, which is to continue reading the email. So instead of starting at the beginning for our opening, we're actually starting in the middle. We want this part to be action packed. So we want very dense wording. We don't want a lot of extra articles and adjectives here. We want things to be very action packed. Lots of words that show them action being taken. We want them to feel like they're right in the middle of the most powerful part of your story. Finally, we want a payoff promise. In this first paragraph, you want to promise them that there's going to be some kind of surprise, some kind of conclusion at the end of this email that they've just got to hear about. And you're going to tease that right here in the opening. So the one, two, three parts of the email actually are that order in their importance. First is the subject. Second is the preheader. ## [09:05 - 12:09]

with that opening where you started with the climax. You started with that big action and you want to close the loop. Have you ever noticed when you listen to a great story or read a great book or watch an incredible movie that the end very closely mirrors the front, but there's been some restoration, some transformation in the middle of the story. But we also want to do one more thing. And that is to tease out that there is more where this came from. We want to suggest that this story is just a small story that's part of a much bigger story.
